news.title: US politicians are trying to change what chatbots say about them
news.text:
WASHINGTON – Dustin Lloyd, a US Democratic primary candidate for Missouri’s state legislature, was a well-known member of his community when he started his political campaign.

But there was one important group that didn’t seem to know him at all: Artificial intelligence chatbots.

If voters quizzed a chatbot like OpenAI’s ChatGPT or Google’s Gemini about Lloyd, they would find only basic information that failed to convey his focus on helping small businesses.

Fortunately for Lloyd, there was a fix. He tweaked his online presence, publishing a Q&A about himself on his campaign website. Later, when the chatbots were quizzed again, they dutifully connected that personal history to his policy goals.

Editing AI’s output proved to be easier than he imagined. Since then, Lloyd said, “I’m constantly working on it every day.”

Candidates have long had to worry about their reputations among voters. Now, they have to worry about what AI thinks about them too. And a new industry has sprung up to help them navigate this world.

Lloyd, 33, relied on a report on his AI presence from CampSight, a tool launched in June by Run for Something Action Fund, a progressive group that recruits young candidates to run for office. It determined that Lloyd’s website and his presence on Wikipedia and Ballotpedia – sites on which voters regularly find information about political figures – were lackluster, advising him to make changes. It even recommended posting threads on Reddit so that the chatbots would begin including information from the online forum in their responses.

“Everyone’s just grasping for some sort of tool to at least inspect these results and ideally influence them,” said Jordan Haines, chief technology officer for Run for Something.

The industry, known as answer engine optimisation, or AEO, is a response to changes in how AI chatbots generate their answers. The earliest chatbots, released several years ago, were trained on huge amounts of information that was already published online. That made many of their answers about news events almost instantly outdated. Modern chatbots solved that problem by searching the internet and pulling in fresh content to answer questions about current events, including the midterms and candidates.

Recommendations from AEO tools like CampSight are typically limited to rewriting content on a candidate’s own website or ensuring Wikipedia is up-to-date. But well-financed candidates may ultimately try to publish a wider array of content online that would be aimed squarely at getting noticed – and parroted – by chatbots, experts said.

“There is a certain degree of panic around it, because we understand it so little and it’s changing so fast,” said Beth Simone Noveck, a professor at Northeastern University who is studying democracy and AI tools, known as large language models or LLMs. “Even if there are people who claim to understand it, even if they master some of the techniques, the changing and unpredictable nature of LLMs just means it’s really difficult to control this process.”

Politicians are also weighing another risk: Getting smeared by AI search results that are not just unflattering but also flat-out wrong. An experiment before Scotland’s parliamentary election in May found that more than a third of AI-generated answers to questions about the upcoming vote were inaccurate. Researchers for Demos, a British think tank, found that multiple chatbots were inventing candidates, fabricating nepotism accusations or concocting financial scandals. Some falsely claimed that an incumbent was running when they were not, while others misstated candidates’ positions.

Still, voters are increasingly turning to chatbots for answers to their political questions. Caucus AI, a political AEO research company, estimated that at least 16 million voters were getting election information from AI, either through chatbots or from AI-generated answers on search results pages.

“As people get more used to this, and as the reliability of the information coming from these chatbots increases, we do think that there will be a significant uptick in 2028 and beyond,” said Meg Schwenzfeier, a founder at Caucus AI and former chief analytics officer of Kamala Harris’ presidential campaign.

Caucus AI ran an experiment to see how long it took for content newly published to Wikipedia to end up in a chatbot’s answer. The result: About 12 minutes. That suggests that candidates can already insert their own information into an AI’s responses by tweaking public information “quickly, unvetted and without anyone on the other side watching”, the company wrote in a blog post about its findings.

Caucus AI is now tracking what chatbots are saying about all Senate, House and gubernatorial candidates, finding that some candidate messaging has an unusual ability to break through. For example, when the group asked three different chatbots about Mary Peltola, the Democratic challenger for US Senate in Alaska, they all cited her unique slogan: “Fish. Family. Freedom.”

“We’re exploring what causes that to get picked up,” Schwenzfeier said. “There’s still a lot we don’t understand, and these models themselves are black boxes.”

If candidates and political groups can nudge chatbots to produce more palatable answers, experts in digital strategy worry that foreign influence operatives and others might also try to manipulate AI searches.

“Disinformation actors are, by definition, early adopters – they will use every technique and every technology they can,” said Tim Chambers, who runs the digital and social media arm of Dewey Square Group, a public affairs firm in Washington whose clients include labour unions and government agencies.

The firm has been helping clients audit their websites to see how accessible they are to chatbot scrapers; in research published in February, Dewey found that far-right sites and sites with “very low” factual integrity had set up their websites to be easily seen by AI tools, compared with outlets that were centre-left or highly factual.

Chambers said that his clients were thinking about “needing to rebuild their websites for machines as much as for humans”, and considering how to show up in chatbot queries posed in multiple languages.

For Lloyd, the Democratic primary candidate in Missouri, the changes he made had an instant impact. When the chatbot was first asked who voters should support in the primary, it recommended his opponent, Tanya Lakins, citing her focus on small businesses. After Lloyd made tweaks to his website, the chatbot changed its tune.

“If you’re voting in the Democratic primary,” it wrote, “Dustin Charles Lloyd appears to have the strongest and most explicit small-business platform.” NYTIMES
news.author: Sph Media

news.title: Who Is Replacing Graham Platner: Maine Democrats Rally Behind Troy Jackson After Chaotic Primary Scramble
news.text: Troy Jackson has all but sealed the Democratic nomination for Maine's high stakes Senate race, after securing commitments from a majority of the delegates set to formally choose the party's candidate next weekend. More than two thirds of the 601 delegates who will attend the statewide nominating convention have already pledged their support to Jackson, even though those pledges aren't legally binding. Jackson posted dominant results at the county level meetings held this past weekend, where delegates were selected, leaving his rivals well behind in the count. That momentum led two of his most organized challengers, Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and former state public health official Nirav Shah, to withdraw from the race entirely on Sunday. A Race Reshaped by ScandalJackson's rise comes after a chaotic stretch for Maine Democrats. The party's statewide convention, set for next weekend in Bangor, will bring an end to a scramble that began when Graham Platner, who had originally won the primary, dropped out earlier this month. That exit followed a Politico report in which a woman who previously dated Platner alleged that he had forced her to have sex with him. Platner has denied the allegations. Who Is Troy JacksonJackson brings a distinct background to the race. He's a fifth generation logger from Allagash, located in Maine's far northern corner, and has spent years building a career organizing progressive activists across the state. He also has a notable connection to Sen. Bernie Sanders of Vermont, who backed Jackson earlier this year during his run for governor. A Tight Timeline Against Susan CollinsWith the nomination effectively in hand, Jackson now faces a steep challenge ahead. He'll have under four months to build a competitive statewide campaign against Republican Sen. Susan Collins, the only GOP senator seeking reelection this cycle in a state that former Vice President Kamala Harris carried back in 2024. Collins has a long track record of fending off well funded Democratic challengers dating back to her first election in 1996. She currently holds roughly $11 million in campaign reserves, based on the most recent filings with the Federal Election Commission, Politico reported. Jackson's late start puts him at an even steeper disadvantage. Maine's nominating convention falls just 101 days before Election Day in November, meaning Jackson will have even less time to campaign against Collins than Harris had in her unsuccessful 2024 run against Donald Trump. Get Latest News Live on Times Now along with Breaking News and Top Headlines from US News and around the World.
news.summary: Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Democratic nomination for Maine's Senate race after winning support from more than two thirds of the state's delegates, with rivals Shenna Bellows and Nirav Shah dropping out.
news.author: Sagar Kar

news.title: Troy Jackson dominates Maine Democratic caucuses, emerges as frontrunner to face Susan Collins
news.text:
Following Platner’s drop-out over allegations that he raped a girlfriend five years ago, Jackson sought to distance himself from Platner personally while hugging the grassroots base that had elevated Platner to win the primary with 72 percent of the vote and outlast a two-term incumbent governor. 

Jackson, meanwhile, finished third in the Democratic primary for governor on the same day.

Jackson clearly wasn’t penalized by activists for his previous link with Planter. In fact, he so dominated county caucuses on Saturday and Sunday that his top two rivals — Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and Nirav Shah, former director of the Maine Center for Disease Control and Prevention — both ended their campaigns. Dan Kleban, co-founder of Maine Beer Company, also dropped his bid and endorsed Jackson. 

In 3 of the state’s 16 counties, Jackson’s supporters won every delegate spot. In five others, he won all but one delegate. That included Cumberland County, the state’s largest by population, where Jackson won 148 out of 149 delegates, with the lone outlier uncommitted.

In total, Jackson amassed 456 out of the 500 elected delegates over the weekend. When added to the 101 existing state committee members who vote, that is well over the 302 needed to secure the nomination at next Saturday’s convention.

If the results seemed swift, it was partly out of necessity and partly due to Jackson’s ability to organize supporters quickly. Maine state law only gave Democrats two weeks to name a replacement. While the convention will formally do so two days before the deadline, Democrats appear to have pulled off the entire process in 12 days— unless something unforeseen happens in the next week.

Jordan Wood, who lost a congressional primary last month, stressed in an interview with the Globe on Sunday that he was vowing to stay in the contest through the convention. According to a Bangor Daily News tabulation, he had only two supporters elected as delegates over the weekend.

It is unclear whether that remains a tenable position for Wood. It is also unclear whether CNN and the Bangor Daily News will proceed with their joint debate on Thursday night, or whether Jackson would even bother participating if they do.

The caucuses themselves were remarkably efficient affairs. Over 17,000 registered Democrats voted in their home county caucuses, according to state party officials. After being verified, each voter received an email and text message with a unique web link to their ballot.

At York County’s caucus on Sunday morning, hundreds filled the Sanford High School theater. For two hours, Democrats mingled, gathered, and checked in at their preferred candidate’s campaign table, if one was set up. Jackson rallied his supporters outside. Then, within 10 minutes of the formal meeting starting, voting got underway without any speeches from the campaigns.

The more organized campaigns, like Jackson’s and Shah’s, offered supporters shirts to wear (dark blue for Jackson, green for Shah) and handed out a slate of campaign-aligned delegates to vote for on the ballot.

This slate-of-delegates approach appeared helpful to voters looking to support a specific Senate campaign. In York County, for example, 444 candidates ran for 84 delegate and 17 alternate delegate positions. Each candidate was listed by name, with no indication of whom they supported. Due to the sheer number of votes to be cast, voters were given 90 minutes to complete their ballots.

Voters could be seen holding a piece of paper with their preferred campaign’s delegate selections in one hand while casting votes on their phones with the other.

As voting began, a line of a few dozen people seeking technical help from party staff and volunteers slowly dissipated after 20 minutes. County party officials noted that many voters stayed home and cast their ballots via phone or computer.

Jackson’s dominant performance will likely pressure the remaining candidates to leave the race to make unifying the party easier ahead of Saturday’s convention.

The snap election process across the state — driven largely by party insiders and activists — appeared to give a significant leg up to candidates who ran for governor a month ago, like Jackson, who had already built statewide organizations over the past year.

As Jackson likely spends the next week trying to unite Democrats behind him and quickly scale up a Senate campaign with roughly 100 days until Election Day, one immediate concern is fundraising. Jackson raised the least money among the major candidates for governor this year.

Republican groups backing Collins have already spent or reserved over $112 million in ads, while Democrats have reserved only half that amount, according to tracking service AdImpact. Given Maine’s critical role in deciding control of the U.S. Senate next year, however, Democrats nationwide could flood the state with funds.

For Democrats to flip the Senate, they must accomplish two goals: win competitive elections in states currently held by Democrats -- namely New Hampshire, Michigan, and Georgia -- and flip four states currently held by Republicans. Maine is the only state with a current Republican senator up for election this year in a state that Kamala Harris won in 2024.

“We know the nation has been watching this wild process up here, but it seems to be coming to a quick conclusion in a way that surprised me,” said Ethan Standel, a 32-year-old Jackson supporter from Saco who voted in person on Sunday.

James Pindell is a Globe political reporter who reports and analyzes American politics, especially in New England.
news.author: James Pindell Globe

news.title: AOC accused of putting on new accent during Memphis rally: 'Going full Kamala'
news.text: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., was accused of putting on an accent during a Memphis rally last week, with critics on social media comparing her to former Vice President Kamala Harris. Ocasio-Cortez was speaking at a campaign rally in Memphis on Friday for progressive U.S. House candidate Justin Pearson, who currently serves as a Tennessee state representative. "We cannot afford to leave Tennessee behind," she said. "We cannot afford to leave Kentucky behind, Alabama behind, Mississippi behind, Georgia behind, South Carolina behind. There is no country where we are split apart." AOC TELLS NEW YORKERS TO ‘PULL UP’ TO ALABAMA DURING RALLY SPEECH BEHIND BULLETPROOF GLASS "About a month or two ago, we were in Montgomery, Alabama — Sister [Rep. Ayanna] Pressley and I were in Montgomery, Alabama, and one of the things that we said was that it is time for the North to roll up to the South," Ocasio-Cortez continued. Critics online singled out this part of her speech, saying she put on a "fake accent." "AOC, from the ‘Bronx’ sounding like a southern Baptist preacher while in Memphis," actor Michael Rapaport, who has said he’ll run for the New York City mayor’s office in 2029, posted on social media. KAMALA HARRIS TORCHES OWN PARTY MEMBERS INVESTED IN HOLDING ON TO THEIR 'TITLE,' ADMITS, 'I AM EXHAUSTED' "Hillary Clinton developed an accent. Kamala Harris developed an accent. AOC is developing an accent. It’s all patronizing," conservative communications strategist Steve Guest posted on social media. "Rep. AOC just got busted putting on a FAKE ACCENT for a predominantly black audience. She's seriously going full Kamala for 2028," another user wrote, referencing a potential presidential bid. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Harris was repeatedly accused of altering her accent depending on the group of people she's speaking to. In 2024, Harris was slammed for using what critics called a fake Southern accent while speaking at a rally in Atlanta in July 2024, and again during a Labor Day rally in Detroit that year. During her 2021 overseas trip to France, critics claimed Harris used a "French accent" while touring a COVID lab.
news.summary: Critics compared Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez to Kamala Harris after her Memphis rally speech, accusing her of putting on a fake southern accent.
news.author: Stephen Sorace

news.title: Auschwitz survivor, 96, in NYC forced to hide Star of David as he gives Dems history lesson on antisemitism
news.text:
It’s a dire case of deja vu!

A 96-year-old Auschwitz survivor said he now hides his Star of David chain because of spiraling antisemitism in the Big Apple as he gave Democrats a brutal history lesson about anti-Jewish hatred.

Jerry Wartski said the Democratic Party and the liberal Democratic Socialists of America are being infiltrated by antisemites who are fueling an environment that has Jews feeling unsafe in the streets as if it were Europe during the rise of the Third Reich.

“You have to keep your eyes open to what’s going on,” Wartski told The Post in a recent interview. “I cover up my Star of David chain under my shirt when I walk outside now.

“It’s the same old story,” said Wartski, whose parents were killed in concentration camps during the Nazis’ systemic slaughtering of Jews in the Holocaust. “I close the shirt. I don’t advertise. This is the `Land of the Free, Home of the Brave?'”

Wartski said he’s been growing uneasy over what he feels is a tangible change in New York and the US, which were once safe havens for Jews who fled the Nazis and oppression in other countries.

Now, Jews could face hostility here or abroad, he added.

“If you run away from New York, you run away from the United States. There’s nowhere else to go,” he said. “Europe is corroded.”

Poland-born Wartski and an older brother survived the Nazi wipeout of two ghettos — his hometown Ozorkow and Lodz — before his family was shipped to Auschwitz, where his mother was gassed to death when he was still a child, he said.

Later, he was relocated to Nordhausen concentration camp in Germany, where his father died just before the American liberation.

Today, he has grown disillusioned with the Democratic Party because it appears to be succumbing to anti-Jewish rhetoric under what he said is a coalition of socialists, communists and radical Islamists.

Wartski blasted democratic socialist Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a staunch pro-Palestinian activist and outspoken critic of Israel, as part of the problem.

“Mamdani is definitely an antisemite — a big one. He doesn’t hide it.” Watski said. “I didn’t vote for him.”

Mamdani, the city’s first Muslim mayor, backs the controversial boycott, divestment and sanctions campaign against Israel, which some view as antisemitic.

Wartski backs President Trump, saying he’s fighting the good fight against the radical Islamists in Iran and supports Republican Bruce Blakeman for governor.

During a Post interview in 2024, he criticized Democrat Kamala Harris for likening Trump to Hitler.

The Holocaust survivor said despite his worries about the state of the city today, he’s lived a good life in New York, running his own sweater-knitting business before launching a career in real estate.

“I was lucky during the war. I was lucky in business. I survived,” he said.
news.summary: He's seen the evil of antisemitism before -- and he's seeing it again! Jerry Wartski, a 96-year-old Holocaust survivor, said he now hides his Star of David chain when he walks New York City's streets because of Jew hatred.
news.author: Carl Campanile

news.title: Courthouse weddings are booming as many couples skip pricey traditional ceremonies
news.text: A number of West Coast couples are reportedly foregoing costly, extravagant weddings these days. Instead, they're favoring simple courthouse ceremonies. The demand for straightforward civil ceremonies is causing a rush for marriage appointments at municipal buildings in California, the Los Angeles Times recently reported. "They pretty much get picked up as soon as we release them," Santa Barbara County Clerk Melinda Greene told the publication. "We have people from all over the world." TAYLOR SWIFT'S WEDDING SPARKS HOLIDAY WEEKEND ETIQUETTE DEBATE FOR GUESTS Slots to get married at California courthouses have been filling up months in advance, while some couples snap up last-minute bookings. Elopement has turned from "a breezy wedding alternative into a formal contact sport," the same publication reported. "It books up so fast, you have to be online at the exact time [of day] you plan on having your appointment," Asha Marshall of So Fetch Photography told the Times. "A lot of my brides get stressed out." Micro-weddings are reportedly on the rise due in part to social media posts inspiring couples to get married at the same venues where celebrities have tied the knot. Santa Barbara’s Spanish Moorish revival courthouse, for instance, is a sought-after spot where Kamala Harris married Doug Emhoff and where Kourtney Kardashian got hitched to Travis Barker. TEST YOURSELF WITH OUR LATEST LIFESTYLE QUIZ "We see dozens a day, starting at 8 o’clock in the morning," Roy Lee, Santa Barbara County supervisor, told the Times. San Francisco City Hall, where Marilyn Monroe and Joe DiMaggio were married with great fanfare in 1954, is a top spot for a courthouse wedding, according to the Times. The venue hosts some 7,000 weddings a year. The trend also coincides with Gen Z — those commonly cited as having been born between 1997 and 2012 — now entering its peak marrying years. The oldest members of Gen Z are hitting the average marrying age of 28, online wedding-planning and registry company Zola reported, and this generation has become a primary driver of the wedding industry. Micro-weddings are reportedly on the rise, due in part to social media. "The 2026 wedding season reflects the broader cultural shifts of this generation," Zola reported in its "2026 Wedding Trends: First Look" report, a survey of more than 11,500 couples getting married in 2026. CLICK HERE FOR MORE LIFESTYLE NEWS "Our couples are using TikTok and AI in their everyday lives, embracing both cocktails and mocktails and prioritizing their mental wellness," the report said. Though cost-friendly and streamlined civil marriage ceremonies are popular in some places, the average cost of a wedding is $36,000, according to Zola — while the average guest count has remained steady at around 145 people, similar to last year. "Even with economic jitters, we’re seeing couples prioritize the celebration they want over other financial goals," the company reported. "The sentiment is clear: 85% of couples say their wedding will be worth every penny." CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP Bank of America said in June 2026 that "wedding-related spending grew 8.5% year-over-year so far in 2026 … suggesting that higher prices haven’t completely curbed spending on that special day." CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR OUR LIFESTYLE NEWSLETTER The report also found consumers are spending less on one-time formal wear purchases, while more economical, lab-grown diamonds continue to gain traction.
news.summary: Marriage appointments at California courthouses are filling up months in advance as couples flock to venues where many celebrities have tied the knot.
news.author: Teresa Mull

news.title: AOC Deploys New Attempt At Southern Charm During Tennessee Campaign Stop
news.text:
Democratic New York Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez broke out a faux Southern accent during a Saturday campaign appearance in Tennessee.

Ocasio-Cortez, seen as a likely challenger for either the 2028 Democratic nomination for president or for the Senate seat held by Democratic New York Sen. Chuck Schumer, accentuated her remarks while campaigning for Democratic Tennessee state Rep. Justin Pearson. Pearson is running for the seat in the U.S. House of Representatives currently held by retiring Democratic Rep. Steve Cohen. Ocasio-Cortez urged Pearson’s supporters to not write off the state, which favors Republicans, while attempting to evoke the dynacism of a preacher. (RELATED: CNN Panel Melts Down After Kevin O’Leary Compares AOC’s Munich Word Salad To Kamala Harris)

AOC, from the “Bronx” sounding like a southern Baptist preacher while in Memphis. pic.twitter.com/TEHd6HbC3X— MichaelRapaport (@MichaelRapaport) July 18, 2026

“We cannot afford to leave Tennessee behind,” Ocasio-Cortez, who was joined by Democratic Massachusetts Rep. Ayanna Pressley, said. “We cannot afford to leave Kentucky behind, Alabama behind, Mississippi behind, Georgia behind, South Carolina behind.”

Ocasio-Cortez’s attempted accent appears similar to the variety of accents then-Vice President Kamala Harris deployed while campaigning for president in 2024 as the Democratic nominee. At one point, Harris used two different accents during separate campaign stops on the same day.

AOC adopted a lower profile after a disastrous performance during a February security conference in Munich when asked about American policy on Taiwan. The democratic socialist lawmaker rambled for 40 seconds about whether the U.S. should send troops to defend the democratic Republic of China if the communist People’s Republic of China attacked the island.

The left-wing Democrat, one of the more famous members of “The Squad,” also repeated comments about the American northern states needing to “roll up to the South” that she drew fire for after making them during a May political appearance in Montgomery, Ala.

AOC: “It is time for the North to roll up to the South.”What? pic.twitter.com/wLsOHfdg9Z— RNC Research (@RNCResearch) July 18, 2026

“We will not be chased out of places anymore, brothers and sisters,” Ocasio-Cortez said. “It’s going take all us together rejecting separation, for us organize and speak life into one another because we will, we will see the day where we will turn the crooked path straight.”
news.summary: ‘Roll up to the South’
news.author: Democratic New York,Harold Hutchison

news.title: New poll reveals what Americans really think of MAGA and it could be bad news for Trump
news.text:
The midterm elections are coming up, but just how MAGA fans feel about their current sitting president and founder of the movement - Donald J Trump – has been revealed. 

Just two days ago it was announced that Trump had joined predecessors Joe Biden and Jimmy Carter with his latest approval rating reveal on inflation, but what about his overall rating? 

The latest inflation approval ratings found Trump's net approval rating on inflation improved slightly to minus-47 points in June, from minus-50 points in May. 

However, when it comes to looking at Trump's popularity, there's a lot of other pieces at play. 

With just over 100 days until the midterm elections, the CNBC All-America Economic Survey showed just what voters think of the job the administration is doing, and if this bodes well for Trump's term. 

The survey polled 1,000 voters from July 8 to July 12 and painted a bleak picture of the likelihood of MAGA being voted for again by previous supporters. 

That's because the poll found that 57 per cent of respondents said they are unlikely to support any self-identified MAGA candidates this year. 

The poll also found 61 per cent of the group feel pessimistic about the current state of the American economy and its future too. 

Despite these numbers, the POTUS has been vocal about his belief that his approval ratings are his highest 'ever', but his rating has been through the ringer, which seems to be caused in part by the US and Israeli a war with Iran. 

Focaldata conducted a poll released on July 6, and reported Trump's approval rating sat at minus 23, which isn't exactly what you'd call the 'highest ever'. 

A CBS News/YouGov poll in May also revealed that there has been a decrease in voter confidence. 

60 per cent of those polled claimed they are now disapproving of Trump’s handling of his job in the White House. 

Then, 54 per cent of white non-college voters confirmed they too disapproved of Trump’s performance, with 61 per cent being unhappy with the work he is doing, and 66 per cent citing the Iran war as being a bad choice. 

The midterm elections are held halfway through a president's four-year term, and are scheduled to commence on November 3. 

In light of the date coming up, former Democratic presidential candidate, Kamala Harris has urged voters to use their vote with determination. 

She claimed Trump is 'scared of your power' and that he 'wants you to believe that your vote does not matter'. 

"He wants you to lose confidence in our electoral system so you stay home this November," she continued in a statement, noting: "He knows how discontent the American people are, and he wants to make sure that you do not vote." 

She told the people: "Because if the people vote in their numbers, Republicans will lose." 

In a final push to get the public to vote to end Trump's reign, she said to make their 'voices heard' and 'put a real check on this corrupt and cruel administration'. 

UNILAD reached out to the White House for comment.
news.summary: Donald Trump previously claimed to have the 'highest' approval rating 'ever' in a Truth Social post
news.author: Britt Jones

news.title: Despite Trump's unpopularity, new poll shows tight race for Congress
news.text:
As President Donald Trump faces near-record-low disapproval ratings, a new Washington Post-Ipsos poll, conducted July 8-13, found that Democrats have a slim advantage over Republicans ahead of November's midterm elections.

Still, Democratic supporters are more motivated to vote. The WaPo-Ipsos poll shows Democrats leading Republicans by 3 percentage points, 48% to 45%, two years after Kamala Harris' 2024 loss to Trump.

The poll also found that Democrats' edge grows to 8 points among those "absolutely certain to vote" as 53% of highly motivated voters support Democrats while 45% support Republicans. Trump has faced low approval numbers amid issues with the economy and the Iran war.

The Post-Ipsos survey contrasted Democrats' current polling outcomes with 2018. Democrats had a 10-point lead in support for Congress ahead of his first presidential midterm election in which Democrats gained 40 U.S. House seats. Republicans maintained their majority in the Senate.

Trump continues to face waning approval numbers. His overall approval rating stands at 37 percent in the WaPo-Ipsos poll. A new Economist/YouGov poll, conducted July 10-13, found that the same number of Americans approve of his job performance.

What Trump family members will attend the FIFA World Cup Final?

House Speaker Mike Johnson currently has a two-vote margin and cannot afford defections as Republicans hope to enact Trump's agenda. Republicans currently have a 53-47 seat majority in the Senate. Partisan gerrymandering has thrust further uncertainty into Democrats' hopes to regain control of both chambers.

The poll found that Democrats are 10% more likely to certainly vote this year than Republicans, which eclipses their two-point edge in 2018.

Contributing: Fernando Cervantes Jr., Zachary Schermele
news.author: Usa Today

news.title: Gad Saad, the doctor who diagnosed the West
news.text:
The pattern kept repeating itself elsewhere. The same activists who were championing transgender athletes seemed to have no empathy left over for the female athletes that trans athletes were displacing, sometimes dangerously. The same voices demanding that we welcome Muslim immigrants with open arms had, for years, precious little to say about the mostly working-class girls who were being terrorized by Pakistani Muslim rape gangs in Britain on an industrial scale. (I also found it extremely disturbing how the same legacy media that couldn’t get enough of the Catholic Church sexual abuse story during the early 2000s have been virtually silent about the Pakistani Muslim sexual abuse story today. The chances that we ever get a Hollywood-produced movie about the United Kingdom’s “grooming” gangs, à la Spotlight, are next to nil.) The very people who pride themselves on defending the vulnerable were somehow managing to see no evil and hear no evil when one of the worst evils imaginable was being inflicted upon one of the most vulnerable groups of people in our society.

It was somewhere in the middle of watching all this that I found the vocabulary for what I’d been sensing. That vocabulary is the product of the mind of Dr. Gad Saad, and it’s the subject of his essential new book, Suicidal Empathy: Dying to Be Kind, which debuted at No. 1 on the New York Times bestseller list.

I had interviewed Saad once before, on the occasion of his 2023 book, The Saad Truth About Happiness: 8 Secrets for Leading the Good Life. That conversation left me with a clear impression: Here was one of the sharpest, wittiest, and most engaging public intellectuals working today — someone who could move from hard behavioral science to cultural commentary to outright comedy in the space of a single sentence. That impression is what sent me back to him for this book, on this subject. I suspected he’d have something worthwhile to say. He did — considerably more than that, actually.

Who is Gad Saad?

Saad was a marketing professor at Concordia University in Montreal from 1994 to 2026, where he held the Concordia University Research Chair in Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences and Darwinian Consumption from 2008 to 2018. He has held visiting professorships at Cornell University, Dartmouth College, the University of California-Irvine, and Northwood University, and is currently in the process of moving to the United States, where, as of this August, he’ll be joining the University of Mississippi as distinguished visiting professor of the school’s Declaration of Independence Center for the Study of American Freedom. (This past year, he was a scholar at the Declaration of Independence Center while on leave from his Concordia University professorship.) Over more than three decades in academia, he has authored more than 75 scientific papers, pioneering the application of evolutionary psychology to marketing and consumer behavior — a career that has earned him, among other honors, the 2015 President’s Media Outreach Award as Concordia’s top global research communicator.

But it’s Saad’s public-facing work that has made him a household name well beyond the academy. His Psychology Today column, “Homo Consumericus,” has amassed over 7.47 million reads across more than 300 pieces. His YouTube show and podcast, The Saad Truth, have racked up more than 50 million views and downloads. His following spans an unusually wide ideological and cultural range — Elon Musk, Joe Rogan, Jordan Peterson, and Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi have all counted themselves among his admirers.

That crossover appeal is not an accident. Saad’s 2020 book, The Parasitic Mind: How Infectious Ideas Are Killing Common Sense, became an international bestseller by diagnosing the West’s susceptibility to what he called “idea pathogens” — bad ideas that spread virally and hijack sound reasoning. Suicidal Empathy is, in a sense, the sequel and the sharpening: a follow-on diagnosis of one specific idea pathogen that Saad now regards as the master key to understanding nearly everything currently going wrong in Western societies.

A book, a term, and a cultural moment

Suicidal Empathy argues that a corrupted, dysregulated form of compassion — empathy that “hyperfires” toward the wrong targets, in Saad’s phrase from our conversation — now drives a staggering share of the West’s worst policy decisions. The book traces the anatomy of the affliction across eight chapters, from “A Good Virtue Gone Bad” through “Blank Slate Felons” to a closing prescription, “Inoculation Against Suicidal Empathy.”

Saad doesn’t pull any punches; he’s pugilistic in the best sense of the term: intellectually and comedically. The book is laced with the kind of caustic wit that has become his signature. He describes reflexive reality-denial as “Ostrich Parasitic Syndrome” (a term originating in Chapter 7 of his The Parasitic Mind). He calls those most thoroughly captured by the affliction “invertebrate castrati.” In one memorably unsparing aside, he describes Kamala Harris as a “lobotomized cackler.” Saad likens the suicidally empathetic to Gregor Samsa’s transformation in Kafka’s The Metamorphosis — people, he writes, who have undergone “a new form of human-to-insect metamorphosis.” I’d add my own Kafka comparison: The suicidally empathetic of the West have been behaving like the title character of “A Hunger Artist,” who starves himself to death performatively, publicly, and entirely unnecessarily. When his lack of appetite is literally killing him, at that point, the only sane thing to do would be to at least try to eat something, anything. As a Jewish mother would say, Force yourself!

The book catalogs the symptoms with a satirist’s eye for the absurd: Dieting and simply not being overweight has been recast as “white supremacy”; how President Joe Biden publicly regretted calling the man who murdered Laken Riley an “illegal” rather than “undocumented”; how the “DIE (Diversity, Inclusion, and Equity) cult” has metastasized to the point where once-fine schools like the University of Michigan are now spending tens of millions of dollars a year on DEI positions.

“Imagine the number of leading professors who could have been hired with that wasted money,” Saad writes. Or imagine how much more affordable universities could make higher education if they stopped paying these “indulgences” (as Saad describes them) to the high priests of Orthodox Wokeism and instead refocused their efforts and resources on actual education.

Saad is particularly incisive on the West’s asymmetric protectiveness. “The West’s reflex,” he writes, “is always to protect Islam irrespective of the unfolding realities.” Why does one specific religion receive this reflexive deference that others do not? Within the mysterious theology of Wokeism, why is it that some religions are more equal than others?

Saad is equally incisive on the linguistic inanities that suicidal empathy uses to camouflage its deleterious, often violent consequences. These are terms that are so Orwellian that they might even shock Orwell himself.

“Gender-affirming care,” Saad notes, launders what is in fact life-altering, often irreversible medical intervention into something anodyne, making one forget the enormous violence that one is inflicting upon oneself—sacrificing one’s ability to reproduce, and thereby killing any of one’s potential future children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren — by undergoing such a procedure. (In that same spirit, I’d nominate “reproductive rights” as another entrant in the genre — a phrase built to obscure the fact that it is authorizing the murder of potential human life.)

And on the wokeist hierarchy that ranks victim groups against one another, Saad paraphrases Orwell’s Animal Farm: “All lives are important but some a lot more so than others.” That same hierarchy produces demands as arbitrary as insisting that a film adaptation of The Odyssey feature more trans actors — a demand no more coherent, he notes, than insisting that the Dallas Mavericks have more Lebanese Jews on their roster.

A unified field theory

What elevates Suicidal Empathy above a mere grievance-cataloging exercise, and what I asked Saad about directly, is its ambition to function as something like a unified field theory in physics: one underlying mechanism that explains a sprawling, seemingly unconnected list of contemporary pathologies, from DEI to open borders to the collapse of common sense around crime and public safety.

I asked Saad whether he had a Eureka! moment in which he came up with the term (and the diagnosis) of “suicidal empathy.” The term’s roots, he told me, trace back further than most readers realize. He used the term in The Parasitic Mind, but its true origin lies even earlier: in the transcript of his testimony before the Canadian Senate on Bill C-16, where he first flagged what he called the miscalibration of empathy. The conceptual scaffolding came from his years in which he was immersed in the work of E.O. Wilson, particularly Wilson’s writing on why communism fails as a human system despite its appeal — “great idea, wrong species,” as Wilson pithily put it — and from Wilson’s book Consilience, a term, Saad noted, that “many people don’t have in their lexicon.” Having spent decades absorbing sociobiology’s central premise — that biology shapes behavior across every species, humans included — Saad found himself, as he put it, hard-wired to look for synthesis. Confronted with DEI, BLM, open-borders policy, calls to “defund the police,” and more, he went looking for the single mechanism beneath all of them, and suicidal empathy is what he found.

That synthesis, he argues, is driven at root by misplaced guilt and what he calls “collective impostor syndrome,” a kind of civilizational self-doubt that twists compassion into self-destruction.

“The West is engaging in civilizational seppuku,” Saad told me, and he was insistent that the framing isn’t merely rhetorical. “This is not just phraseology.”

The Western mind, in his account, has been “hijacked by parasitic thinking” in a way that causes the empathy instinct to fire reflexively toward the wrong. Saad was candid that different societies sit at different points along this trajectory. Much of Europe, he believes, is far further gone than the United States. America benefits, he told me, from “a set of built-in inoculations,” chiefly our First Amendment, that have slowed the disease’s progress here. “But that doesn’t mean the U.S. is inoculated from it,” he cautioned.

Saad pointed to Dearborn, Michigan, as an early marker, with Paterson, New Jersey, potentially next, and Minnesota after that. Extrapolate a century forward, he suggested, and the number of American Dearborns and Patersons could multiply considerably. There are some grounds for optimism, he noted, that this trajectory can still be reversed — but he was blunt about the obstacles: “I see no evidence that the West is willing to implement” the corrective measures that would actually be required to root out the mental parasite that is suicidal empathy.

As for when the parasite first took hold, Saad traces a genealogy stretching back roughly a century, to the rise of cultural relativism, with postmodernism entering the bloodstream about 50 years ago, and other contributing strains arriving more recently — a constellation of factors that had to align before suicidal empathy could metastasize into the dominant force in Western societies that Saad believes it has become.

The book and the term are already having a major impact on political and sociocultural discourse. Elon Musk has been enthusiastically promoting the book on X (the book bears his endorsement as well) in posts that reach tens of millions of people. The term has already migrated into political discourse abroad — Saad cited its uptake among politicians, political pundits, and cultural commentators.

Toward the end of our conversation, Saad lamented how our society has fallen to such a state where books such as Suicidal Empathy have become necessary. It is, he said, “so regrettable that it even requires me to write these books,” referring to both this one and The Parasitic Mind before it. As he put it, all of the problems that these books seek to address “have been self-inflicted.”

It’s a fitting image, and one I’d extend a bit further: It’s rather like being treated in a hospital for self-inflicted wounds. We can thank the doctor profusely for his skill in stitching us back up, but we’re the ones who did the cutting. Ideally, we’d never have cut ourselves at all. Since we have, we should be grateful that a medic as perspicacious as Saad is in the emergency room to treat the wound. It now falls to the rest of us, our institutions and our politicians included, to make sure we stop inflicting new ones.

Infinite tolerance or survival?

This past academic year, I served as a visiting scholar at Rutgers University, where I taught courses on the history of antisemitism. Saad’s book, and the framework it offers, would sit comfortably on that syllabus if I were to teach the course again. It isn’t just that Suicidal Empathy explains the specific asymmetry I kept encountering — sympathy for black victims but silence for Jewish ones, solidarity with trans athletes but indifference toward the women sidelined by them, protectiveness toward Muslim immigrant communities but willful blindness toward the girls victimized by Pakistani rape gangs. It’s that Saad’s book supplies the single mechanism underlying it all: a wokeist hierarchy in which, per his Orwellian paraphrase, some people really are more equal than others.

Saad closes his book with a line that is destined to outlast the news cycle that produced it: “A society dies when it cares more about exhibiting infinite tolerance and empathy than invoking its survival instinct. It truly is that simple.”

Daniel Ross Goodman (@DanRossGoodman) is a Washington Examiner contributing writer and teaches theology and religious studies at St. John’s University. His next book, Dante’s Guide to Life: How The Divine Comedy Can Change Our Fortunes, Our World, and Ourselves, will be published this fall by Angelico Press.
news.summary: About 10 years ago, I was quite sympathetic — empathetic, even — to the Black Lives Matter movement in its early days. That sympathy ran so deep that the first book I ever published, A Single Life, was a 2020 novel about a black Jewish protagonist, a character built to sit at the intersection of […]

news.title: Key races to be determined by minority of voters
news.text:
Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor, 65 state legislative seats, Office of Hawaiian Affairs trustees and local races in four Hawaii counties will likely be decided by a small share of registered voters — in a midterm election that’s unlikely to spark a surge in voter turnout.

Gov. Josh Green faces no high-profile challenge in his bid for a second term. And neither of Hawaii’s two U.S. senators are up for election this year.

So the marquee primary contests this year remain the Democratic nomination races for lieutenant governor and for the U.S. House seat to represent urban Honolulu.

U.S. Rep. Jill Tokuda has no high-profile Democratic challenger in the Aug. 8 Democratic Party primary to represent rural Oahu and the neighbor islands in Congress. Her likely opponent in the Nov. 3 general election appears to be Republican state Sen. Brenton Awa (R, Kaneohe--Mokuleia), who is making his first statewide run for political office.

The two most closely watched primary showdowns will be U.S. Rep. Ed Case’s reelection bid to represent urban Honolulu in Congress against state Sen. Jarrett Keohokalole (D, Kaneohe-Kailua), and state Rep. Della Au Belatti versus Kauai Mayor Derek Kawakami to become Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor. Bellati shook up Hawaii’s 2026 election in May when she dropped her long-shot bid to also challenge Case and instead jumped into the lieutenant governor’s race.

Kawakami appeared to have a relatively clear path to victory in his first statewide race until Belatti jumped in. She also has never launched a statewide campaign.

There are also competitive down-ballot races with all 51 state House seats and 14 of 25 Senate seats on the ballot and some County Council races that could spur political change.

Honolulu City Council Chair Tommy Waters faces a rematch against former Council member Trevor Ozawa, who lost a close and bitter race against Waters in 2019 that went all the way to the Hawaii Supreme Court, which ruled in Waters’ favor.

A separate lawsuit Ozawa filed last month remains pending, with a hearing scheduled July 27 before First Circuit Judge James Ashford. The complaint argues that Waters’ eligibility under the City Charter does not allow him to seek a third consecutive term. Ozawa also has filed a motion for summary judgment to hasten his lawsuit against Waters.

Former Council Chair Ikaika Anderson also is running for an at-large seat as a trustee for the Office of Hawaiian Affairs.

And there are other familiar names.

Former Kauai Mayor Bernard Carvalho Jr. is running for his old seat.

As is former state Rep. John Mizuno, who resigned his Kalihi House seat after 17 years in 2024 to serve as Gov. Josh Green’s homeless coordinator.

Green then named Mizuno’s wife and office manager, May Mizuno, to serve out the remainder of his term. But May Mizuno lost her first election bid two years ago when she was defeated by Rep. Ikaika Hussey.

Now John Mizuno will challenge Hussey in the Democratic Party primary to again represent Kamehameha Heights--Kalihi Valley.

Former state Rep. Bob McDermott, who served separate long stints in the House, is challenging a fellow Republican, state Sen. Kurt Fevella, to represent Ewa Beach-Ocean Pointe--Iroquois Point.

McDermott ended his second House term to unsuccessfully run to represent the neighbor islands and rural Oahu in Congress. He returned to the House in 
2012 only to resign again to lose a 2022 U.S. Senate race against Democrat Sen. Brian Schatz. McDermott lost again in 2024 as he tried to unseat Democratic U.S. Sen. Mazie Hirono.

State Rep. Jackson Sayama (D, Wilhelmina Rise-Maunalani Heights-St. Louis Heights) gave up his House seat this year to challenge veteran Sen. Les Ihara to represent Palolo-Kaimuki--Moiliili in the state Senate.

Two of Hawaii’s most colorful but so-far unsuccessful political candidates — former UFC mixed martial arts champion BJ Penn and comedian Bu La‘ia Hill — also have reentered the political arena.

In 1994, Hill ran for governor in the Democratic primary against eventual winner Ben Cayetano, and again in 2002 in the general election as a Natural Law Party nominee against Republican winner Linda Lingle.

Hill is now running his third race for governor as a nonpartisan candidate.

In 2004, Hill — whose real name is Shawn Kaui Hill — was running for OHA when he was sentenced to probation and community service for resisting arrest in an altercation at a Hilo flooring store. The following year a judge agreed to allow a deferral, and the convictions were removed from his record after he completed his probation.

Penn is running for Hawaii County’s District 1 Council seat. In 2022, Penn ran unsuccessfully to become the Republican nominee for governor.

Penn’s criminal record includes assaults, drunken driving and a no-contest plea in 2005 to assaulting a police officer. He also had multiple arrests in Hilo last year related to allegations of domestic abuse against his mother, refusal to comply with a lawful stay-away order and violating a temporary restraining order. Those cases are set for an 8:30 a.m. hearing on Aug. 28 in Hilo before Circuit Court Judge Peter Kubota.

There is little expectation that voters will turn out in droves for the party primaries following the 2024 presidential election year, when only 32.3% of all of Hawaii’s registered voters bothered to vote in the primary, a record low since widespread mail-in voting began in 2020.

The 2022 primary attracted only 39.8% of registered voters in a race that ultimately gave Hawaii voters a choice in the following November general election race between then-Lt. Gov. Josh Green and former Lt. Gov. Duke Aiona. The Democratic primary featured a bitter three-way fight between Green, former first lady Vicky Cayetano and former U.S. Rep. Kai Kahele, who now chairs the Office of Hawaiian Affairs.

The turnout in the last two primary elections suggests that residents across the islands remain motivated to register to vote, but most do not bother to actually cast their ballots.

Voter enthusiasm across the state has jumped when the stakes are high.

The anemic August 2024 turnout of 32.3% in the primary race soared to 60.7% in the subsequent November 2024 general election that saw then-former President Donald Trump and Vice President Kamala Harris battling to become president.

Before, in the November 2008 general election, a whopping 66% of registered Hawaii voters turned out when Oahu-born U.S. Sen. Barack Obama beat Republican Sen. John McCain to become president.
news.summary: Hawaii’s next lieutenant governor, 65 state legislative seats, Office of Hawaiian Affairs trustees and local races in four Hawaii counties will likely be decided by a small share of registered voters — in a midterm election that’s unlikely to spark a surge in voter turnout.
news.author: Dan Nakaso

news.title: Democrats toe a line on Israel as AIPAC’s grip on Congress starts to slip
news.text:
For decades, the American Israel Public Affairs Committee operated as the quiet enforcer of a bipartisan consensus: Politicians of both parties supported Israel — AIPAC made sure of it — and the U.S.-Israel relationship was widely understood to be a non-issue in campaigns.

That arrangement publicly ruptured this week with a House floor vote that split Democrats, including their leadership, on whether to cut off military aid to Israel over its actions in Gaza and the West Bank.

The amendment was symbolic. Lawmakers in both chambers have rejected similar versions for years, and this one, like the others, was never going to pass.

But the numbers voting in favor of such measures have surged in tandem with the mounting Democratic primary wins by candidates who built their campaigns on opposing Israel’s war in Gaza, often over AIPAC-backed opponents. 

In May, a record 40 Senate Democrats voted against further military aid to Israel, a roll call that would have been unthinkable two cycles ago. On Wednesday, 103 out of 211 Democrats voted similarly on the House amendment. 

Some of those voting against the amendment to cut $3.3 billion in security aid to Israel, including Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries, D-N.Y., said they had concerns about its scope and political ramifications but agreed with its message.

Some of those voting in favor of cutting off aid, including Democratic Whip Katherine Clark and former Speaker Nancy Pelosi, said they didn’t actually want it to pass but cast their votes to mark the need for a fundamental shift in U.S. policy toward Israel.

“For the good of the Israeli people and the Palestinian people, it is clear that U.S. policy must change,” Pelosi said after her vote. “The United States must be a force for security and stability. The American people are rightly demanding an end to a perpetual cycle of war, and the Netanyahu government cannot maintain its current course.”

In April, a Pew Research Center survey found that 80% of Democrats now hold an unfavorable view of Israel, up from 69% a year earlier and 53% in 2022. Those feelings are showing up at the ballot box in Democratic primaries: 

In New Jersey, progressive organizer Analilia Mejia ran openly against AIPAC and won her February special election for a New Jersey congressional seat after AIPAC affiliates spent millions on attack ads targeting a relatively moderate candidate, former Rep. Tom Malinowski. Then in May, Army veteran and physician Adam Hamawy, who operated on children in Gaza, beat out a crowded field of candidates hoping to replace Rep. Bonnie Watson Coleman, who announced her retirement last year. Hamawy had the backing of American Priorities, a new pro-Palestine super PAC founded to counter the power of AIPAC.

In Illinois, Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss, who is Jewish, won a House primary in March after explicitly criticizing AIPAC; the lobbying group spent at least $5 million trying to defeat him before dialing back its attacks in the final days, when it became clear his replacement could be a candidate still more critical of Israel. 

In Denver, attorney and first-time candidate Melat Kiros defeated longtime Rep. Diana DeGette, a progressive Democrat backed by AIPAC, by making Gaza advocacy a centerpiece of the campaign. 

In New York last month, a trio of House candidates endorsed by Mayor Zohran Mamdani triumphed: Former city comptroller Brad Lander ousted Rep. Dan Goldman, a two-term Brooklyn Democrat endorsed by AIPAC, in a contest between two men who embrace the terms “liberal” and “Zionist.” Union organizer Claire Valdez snagged the nomination for an open seat encompassing parts of Queens and Brooklyn, saying “free Palestine, organize your union and join DSA” in her victory speech. And in Upper Manhattan, first-time candidate Darializa Avila Chevalier — a Gaza activist who attended a pro-Palestinian rally in Times Square the day after Hamas killed roughly 1,200 people, the overwhelming majority of them Israeli — defeated Rep. Adriano Espaillat, the chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus, who was backed by an AIPAC-affiliated super PAC. 

Asked about its recent losses in primaries, AIPAC referred MS NOW to the group’s statement from June: “While disappointed that some of our endorsed candidates did not prevail, our community is proud to support pro-Israel Democrats and Republicans who stand for our values, and we are encouraged that voters in races across the country this primary season continue to choose serious, thoughtful leaders who support a strong U.S.-Israel partnership.”

The next major test comes in August, in Michigan’s Democratic Senate primary, where AIPAC-backed Rep. Haley Stevens faces Abdul El-Sayed, a Mamdani-aligned progressive endorsed by Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez. AIPAC has spent more than $49 million on Stevens’ behalf, while El-Sayed has repeatedly ruled out AIPAC money.

The Democratic referendum

For a generation of Democratic politicians, AIPAC’s endorsement was an asset and its opposition a campaign-ending threat. Criticism of the group or its mission risked being labeled antisemitic.

“Twenty years ago, everybody acknowledged how powerful AIPAC was and hardly anyone was willing to take it on or defy what it wanted. There was even something of a taboo talking about it,” said Stephen Walt, a professor of international affairs at the Harvard Kennedy School and co-author of “The Israel Lobby and U.S. Foreign Policy.”

Now, for a growing share of the party’s electorate, that calculus has reversed. For some Democratic voters, AIPAC’s involvement in a race is itself a reason to vote against the candidate it backs. A candidate’s public opposition to both AIPAC and to Israel’s policies, on the other hand, is seen by some as a sign of “moral clarity and moral courage,” said Usamah Andrabi, communications director at Justice Democrats, the progressive PAC that helped elect Ocasio-Cortez.

AIPAC remains influential, but “it’s no longer an unchallengeable leviathan,” Walt said, likening it to any other domestic lobby, such as the National Rifle Association.

“It’s pretty open now to ask serious questions about the nature of the U.S. relationship with Israel, the amount of support that we give it — both military aid and diplomatic backing,” said Walt, who has had to defend his work against criticism from pro-Israel groups.

People protest during a Pro-Palestinian demonstration near the White House on March 7, 2024.

For Democrats, the reckoning began in 2024, when President Joe Biden’s campaign was dogged by protesters pressing his administration to push for a ceasefire in Gaza and an embargo on sending arms to Israel.

The human toll of Israel’s response to the Hamas attack — which killed more than 20,000 Palestinians in Gaza within the first three months, a toll that has since reached at least 73,000, according to Gaza’s Health Ministry — spurred the creation of the Uncommitted movement, which sought to register disapproval of Biden’s Middle East policies by urging Democratic voters to support “uncommitted” rather than Biden on their primary ballots. After he withdrew from the presidential race, many in the movement carried their opposition forward in declining to support Vice President Kamala Harris.

Then came Mamdani, who built his 2025 New York mayoral campaign in part on open support for Palestinians and opposition to U.S. military aid to Israel. He did not flinch from calling Israel’s actions in Gaza a genocide, and went from polling at 1% to winning by 9 points in the city with the largest Jewish population in the country.

Mamdani’s win was more than a long shot victory. It was evidence that a pro-Palestinian message could be an asset rather than a liability in a Democratic primary. The message for the rest of the party was hard to miss: On Israel, the primary electorate had begun rewarding the candidates willing to criticize its policies, and punishing those who wouldn’t.

Then-New York State Rep. Zohran Mamdani speaks during a news conference outside the White House on Nov. 27, 2023, to announce a hunger strike to demand that President Joe Biden call for a permanent ceasefire and no military aid to Israel.

That pressure has reshaped how some Democratic elected officials talk, even those closer to the ideological center. Gov. Gavin Newsom of California, a prominent 2028 White House prospect, has been visibly recalibrating. In a March “Pod Save America” interview, he likened Israel to an “apartheid” state and said the U.S. had no choice but to reconsider military support.

Weeks later, he told Politico he regretted the word and drew a line many in the party, like Pelosi, are trying to draw: “I revere the state of Israel. I’m proud to support the state of Israel. I deeply, deeply oppose Bibi Netanyahu’s leadership.” On accepting AIPAC money, he was unequivocal: “Never have and never will.”

There are still plenty of Democrats who proudly accept AIPAC’s support and win with it. Rep. Greg Landsman of Ohio raked in $593,169 from AIPAC-affiliated groups and individuals this cycle. His primary opponent tried to tag him “Genocide Greg” for his full-throated support of Israel; Landsman carried 68% of the vote.

Although Landsman speaks passionately about the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, which he calls “awful,” his voting record reflects continued support for Israel.

“I just think it’s really important for Israel to maintain its ability to defend itself. And sometimes it means getting in front of issues as opposed to sitting there and waiting for people to attack you,” Landsman told MS NOW.

But he too pointed to Netanyahu rather than Israel itself as the problem, and agreed that “there should be a debate” about what the U.S.-Israel “partnership looks like.”

The Republican shift

Rep. Thomas Massie speaks with supporters after his concession speech on May 19, 2026 in Hebron, Ky.

The change has not been confined to the left. A month ago in Kentucky, Rep. Thomas Massie lost his primary to Ed Gallrein, a former Navy SEAL who was backed by Trump and was the beneficiary of more than $9 million in AIPAC-affiliated spending. Massie — a seven-term incumbent and one of the few Republicans willing to break publicly with Trump — wanted everyone to know who he blamed.

Facing his supporters on primary night, Massie opened with a joke about the man who had just defeated him. “I would have come out sooner,” the Kentuckian said, “but I had to call my opponent and concede, and it took a while to find Ed Gallrein in Tel Aviv.”

The line drew laughs. But it also touched on an old and ugly insinuation — that a rival’s true loyalties lie abroad — even as it captured something real about a fault line now running through the Republican coalition.

Massie, who sponsored the most recent amendment to cut off aid to Israel, drew the ire of AIPAC, which denounced him as a troublemaker seeking to undermine U.S.-Israeli relations.

“We are deeply appreciative of the 314 Republicans and Democrats who voted to reject Thomas Massie’s latest anti-Israel effort and are disappointed by the 103 Democrats who voted with Massie to weaken America and our ally Israel,” AIPAC posted. “This so-called messaging vote on Israel’s security sends a dangerous signal to both our allies and our enemies around the world.” 

Some of the conditions that produced the New York sweep are also contributing to this: a Republican electorate that, while still broadly supportive of Israel, is fracturing at its generational edges. That same Pew Research Center survey from April found that 57% of Republicans under 50 now view Israel unfavorably, up from 50% the year before; in both parties, majorities of voters under 50 now rate Israel negatively.

A bloc of prominent MAGA figures — former Fox News host Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly and former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene among them — have broken with Trump in his second term. Among the reasons, by their own account, is that they no longer believe the U.S. should support Israel unconditionally.

There are notable differences in the forces reshaping the two parties, however.

Democrats are responding to a base that increasingly views the war in Gaza as a moral failure; Republicans are splintering along generational lines and around a MAGA media ecosystem newly willing to question the alliance. 

But the two currents converge on the same institution: For decades, AIPAC helped set the boundaries of acceptable debate over Israel in American politics. Those boundaries are now being tested from both sides at once — and increasingly, they are not holding.
news.author: Zeeshan Aleem

news.title: Vance-Rubio or Rubio-Vance Is a Spectacular Ticket
news.text:
Many Conservatives have long been concerned that once Donald Trump's second term comes to a close, the GOP might not be able to win in 2028, especially in the face of unending leftist voter fraud schemes. With the revelations and supporting documentation that President Trump made available on Thursday evening, reasonably fair elections now seem possible.

Superstars on Deck

So, who will head the GOP ticket in 2028? The two leading candidates for president, each outstanding, include U.S. Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Either man would make an excellent president.

Currently, the scale tilts toward JD Vance, with Marco Rubio possibly as vice president. JD Vance has proven himself over and over again, at home and abroad, with sympathetic audiences and with hostile audiences. Though he will only be 44, and that might be seen as a detriment, if he wins two terms, he'll step down at 52 and be a senior statesman for the rest of his life.

Meanwhile, Marco Rubio, who has periodically scoffed at the idea of running for president in 2028, has already shown himself to be among the best Secretaries of State the U.S. has ever had. His international acumen is astounding. In two to three minutes, or less, he can eloquently and succinctly explain the Trump Administration’s objectives regarding any nation or region of the earth. By all indicators, he will make an excellent vice president ...or president, and while some news reports say that he has already bowed out for 2028, in politics things can change quickly.

What's best? Marco Rubio should head the ticket, and JD Vance should serve as VP, even if it means that Vance would be in that post for eons. Marco Rubio is 55 years old and, in 2029, if he serves as VP to a younger man, potentially for two terms, it might dampen his chances to be president in the future.

With Marco Rubio as POTUS, even if JD Vance ends up being in the VP position for another four or eight years, the positive effects that he will have and the messages he can continue to spread will be valuable both to the U.S. and to the world.

After Rubio's run, whether it's four years or eight years, JD Vance would still be young enough to run for president. Indeed, in 2032 he'll be 48 and in 2036 he'll only be 52. Compare that to President Trump, who's now 80, or to Joe Biden, bless his heart, who was 82 and two months when he left office. In contrast, in 2036 JD Vance is still a spring chicken.

A Blank Slate

Who might these GOP superstars run against? Hopefully it's the loony Democrat Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (NY-14), although California Gov. Gavin Newsom is more likely. Newsom is two-faced and has so many skeletons in the closet that during his campaign he'll likely falter as badly as Kamala Harris did.

Many on the Right fervently wish that the DNC would install Kamala Harris back at the top of their ticket, but the chances are slim. Top donors have already pulled far away from her, and she is viewed as a liability more than anything else.

Who else might be on the horizon for the Democrats? It's hard to say because of the lack of leadership and since the party has been taken over by far-left Socialists, even popular Democrats have taken a hit in terms of their favorability. The most visible, as well, won’t do: Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT) will be way too old. Hakeem Jeffries is not viable nationally. Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker will become a slow-moving joke if he embarks on the campaign trail, even worse than Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz.

Congressmen and congresswomen running for president generally do not fare well. Most Democrat senators are feckless. The Democrats’ best approach could be to pick a successful governor, perhaps Josh Shapiro of Pennsylvania or Andy Beshear of Kentucky.

GOP Superstars 

Both JD Vance and Marco Rubio have punched the right tickets and have repeatedly demonstrated their ability to lead on a national and global stage. This dynamic duo cannot be matched by any two that the Democrats could run. Even a significant portion of rank-and-file Democrats realize that. The rest have been hopelessly brainwashed by the fakestream media.

November 2028 is still 28 months away, and we first have to score big in the 2026 midterms, following the November 2025 debacle. As a prelude to these critical junctures, as more and more illegal aliens are deported, inflation levels off, Middle East peace prospects brighten, the chance of massive voter fraud is diminished, and all of the pledged investments in America that Donald Trump has generated break ground, GOP prospects improve across the board.

Each of us needs to stay vigilant and influence family, friends, and associates in at least little ways, consistently, for the duration. We don't want to be complacent or to take our foot off the gas pedal.

news.title: Michael Goodwin: Vance’s baffling statements on Israel & Epstein should disqualify him from 2028 GOP ticket
news.text:
From the moment in 2024 when Donald Trump picked JD Vance to be his running mate, I believed it was a good move. 

Vance, a first-term Ohio senator, was popular among Trump’s base, which is why he was given the task of concentrating on Midwest swing states during the campaign.

His surgical dismantling of blowhard Tim Walz during the veep debate was a thing of beauty, and it bears remembering that Pennsylvania, Michigan and Wisconsin all flipped for Trump.

While the president clearly deserves most of the credit, Vance certainly did his part.

I’ve also viewed Vance as a solid vice-president. 

He’s been loyal, smart, calm and persistent, making him an effective opponent for the leftist media cabal during interviews.

For those and other reasons, it’s not a surprise that most early polling has him leading the GOP field to be Trump’s successor and win the 2028 nomination. 

The very capable and savvy Secretary of State Marco Rubio is running a close second.

‘Who is this guy?’

Alas, the appearance of smooth sailing in all things Vance came to a crashing end with his shocking interview with Joe Rogan last week. 

His comments on Israel alone have me asking, “Who is this guy?”

And not in a nice way.

He was so bad that I entertained the wild possibility he was floating the idea of switching to the Democratic Party.

That’s because his acid comments about the Jewish state echoed some antisemitic themes, and happened to come on the same day that half of House Dems took the extraordinary step of voting to end all aid to Israel, military and humanitarian.

The move failed because of strong Republican support for the Jewish state. 

Yet at the same time, here was the Republican vice president of an administration that has twice joined Israel in a war against Iran bad mouthing our crucial ally in ways that rhyme with the conspiracy theories of the Dems’ socialist wing.

Specifically, Vance accused people “within Israel’s system” of trying to manipulate American public opinion to prolong the war with Iran “indefinitely.”

That is not far from the false charge by The New York Times and others that Trump was persuaded by sinister Israeli pressure to enter the war, as if he had never said he would not allow Iran to get a nuclear bomb.

Vance also bemoaned what he called a heavily funded Israeli campaign to derail the negotiations with Tehran, which he is now leading.

“I know beyond a shadow of a doubt that there have been people within the Israeli government who are trying to, like, actually shift us away from [negotiations] because they want to continue the military campaign,” Vance said.

“Again, not towards any objective, but just indefinitely.”

Addressing those criticizing his approach, presumably in Israel, he said: “Go to hell. I’m going to do what I have to do for the American people. I represent Americans first.”

That was bad enough, but he wasn’t finished. Vance also wandered deep into the woods of Jeffrey Epstein theories, admitting he was among the “Epstein conspiracy theorists.”

He proved it by declaring, without offering any evidence, that the dead pedophile “had connections to the upper, the highest levels, of American intelligence. He clearly had connections to the highest levels of Israeli intelligence,” Vance told Rogan.

Deep-web echoes

Although he cited the known Epstein association with Ehud Barak, a former Israeli prime minister, he didn’t note that Barak insisted he engaged in no wrongdoing and said he regrets the relationship with Epstein.

Vance didn’t stop there, saying, strangely that “As much as I know, you know, Prime Minister Netanyahu, not a particularly popular person in the United States of America right now, Epstein seemed to be connected to the elements of the Israeli deep state that were left of center.”

His remarks are not far from the belief in deep-web dungeons that Epstein was a Mossad agent.

On a roll, Vance added, that, in America, Epstein was “connected across the board, he had Republican friends, he had Democratic friends. 

He had much deeper connections to the Israeli left of center than right of center.”

He wisely did not mention his boss, whose long relationship with Epstein has convinced Dems there is political profit to be made by lobbing endless accusations about the president.

The closest Vance came was when he confessed the administration “absolutely screwed up” its communications of the Epstein files, and singled out former Attorney General Pam Bondi.

He said Bondi’s claim about having binders of documents on her desk “made people mistrust the entire effort,” adding that Bondi “overstated what we had and what we didn’t have.”

The incident surely played a key role in Trump booting Bondi.

Vance’s turn against Israel reminds me of a conversation several months ago with a friend who is active in Jewish causes. 

A long-time donor and supporter of Vance, he mentioned reports he was hearing that the veep was sounding like Tucker Carlson, a close Vance friend who broke with Trump and routinely denounces Israel.

Although I knew that Carlson had lobbied Trump to make Vance his running mate, my friend’s warning about a creeping anti-Israel bias by Vance didn’t sound right to me.

That was my mistake.

Ridiculous comparison

With Trump making near-daily headline-grabbing comments on the war, I missed that Vance last month used a ridiculous comparison to describe Israel’s conflict with Hezbollah in Lebanon. 

He called it a “chicken and egg problem” and said all nations have a right to defend themselves.

Stop right there — Hezbollah is not a nation. It is an occupying force of terrorists in Lebanon to further Iran’s goal of eliminating Israel, and that’s why Israeli forces are trying to root it out.

And that is why Israel objected to America’s willingness to include its Hezbollah conflict in the cease-fire.

I would expect such an ignorant view from a Kamala Harris, but how could Trump’s vice president not understand the essential facts?

The Rogan interview convinces me that Vance really has moved far along the Carlson path.

When it comes to Israel, he’s closer to Dems than to the heart of the GOP.

For example, a Washington Free Beacon poll shows that likely Republican primary voters remain deeply supportive of the war and Trump’s handling of it.

The outlet reported that those voters want a 2028 candidate who supports a strong US-Israel relationship by a margin of 57 percentage points. 

They also want one who forcefully denounces antisemitism by a margin of 52 percentage points.

As of now, JD Vance is not that guy.
news.summary: JD Vance, a first-term Ohio senator, was popular among Trump’s base, which is why he was given the task of concentrating on Midwest swing states during the campaign.
